letters.

Circumstance No. 2
UPT]BLIC GEI{ERALLYII EXCEPTION
The city Council is naking a decision.
The decision will have a naterial financial effect
on an economic interest of councilmember x.
The decision will also effect a number of persons in
the public in the same manner that it will effect
the councilroember.
wheD does the lpublic generally't exceptlon aPPtY?
The FPPC regulations provide that even if a decision would have a
material financial effect on an economic interest of Councilmember
X, the Council,member is not disqualified if the decision will
affect in substantially the same manner:
1. 
The councilmenberrs interesti and
2. 
The interests of the public generally or a significant
segment of the public in the jurisdiction.
The FPPC acknowledges that it has never adopted a test for
deternining what constitutes the I'public generally" or 
a
ttsignif icant segTment of the public.rt Therefore, there is no set
formula to determine when the exception applies.
The FPPC advises that a deternination of whether the public
generally exception is applicable depends on the particular facts
of a specific decision. 
I suggest that the rrpublic qenerallytl

sections.question_synthetic: This document is an attorney's memorandum to city councils summarizing FPPC informal advice on conflict of interest disqualification rules for councilmembers in various zoning and project decision scenarios.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is incoming correspondence from an attorney to FPPC counsel referencing prior informal advice letters (not the FPPC response itself). The document lacks formal Q&A structure; instead it contains a client memorandum summarizing FPPC guidance on multiple scenarios. Low confidence due to unclear whether this is the actual informal advice or the attorney's independent summary. Document appears truncated at end.

embedding.summary: A city attorney (Roger W. Kraiuel) forwards a memorandum to three California cities (Coronado, Del Mar, Encinitas) summarizing FPPC informal advice on conflict of interest disqualification standards for councilmembers, covering four specific circumstances: rezoning property, the public generally exception, project alternative site selection, and geographic distance measurement.
